name: .NET Edit-and-Continue
summary: Visual Studio's feature: modify source code while a debug session is paused, continue execution with the new code applied. CLR's Profiling API exposes `ICorProfilerInfo::SetILFunctionBody` for IL replacement. Restricted to Debug builds, certain edit categories. JVM HotSwap via JVMTI is the Java analogue.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: .NET's **Edit-and-Continue (EnC)** is Visual Studio's feature that lets a developer modify source code while a paused debug session is active and continue execution with the new code applied. The CLR's Profiling API exposes `ICorProfilerInfo::SetILFunctionBody`, allowing the debugger to replace method IL at the bytecode level mid-execution.

sections.title: Constraints and trade-offs
sections.content:
The implementation constraints are substantial:

- EnC only works in Debug builds (so optimisation can't have inlined or eliminated the target method).
- Only certain categories of edits are supported: method body changes yes, signature changes no, adding/removing fields no.
- The new IL must be re-JIT-compiled before the next call; the old version is invalidated.

Despite these limits, EnC remains one of the most-used Visual Studio features for iterative debugging. The analog in Java is **HotSwap** via the JVMTI (JVM Tool Interface): debuggers can replace method bodies in a running JVM, subject to similar constraints. IntelliJ IDEA uses this for its "Reload Changed Classes" feature.

For a language designer wanting EnC-style support: the runtime must support re-JIT of methods, the debugger protocol must support code replacement, and the optimisation pipeline must distinguish debug builds (which preserve method boundaries) from optimised builds (which inline freely).
